queue.script: Why is there a literal castle in Toronto? Casa Loma was built in the early nineteen hundreds by a wealthy businessman who wanted a European style mansion. It ended up becoming one of the most unique landmarks in the city.

There’s more under Yonge Street than just pipes. Beneath Yonge Street, you’ll find subway tunnels, utility systems, and parts of the underground PATH network. It’s basically a hidden city beneath one of the busiest streets in the world.

Those arches at Nathan Phillips Square aren’t just for looks. They’re part of the structure of City Hall and help support the elevated walkway above. So next time you’re there, you’re standing under a key part of Nathan Phillips Square.

Union Station is way bigger than it looks. Under Union Station, there are layers of train platforms, tunnels, and connections to the PATH system. It’s a massive underground network moving thousands of people every day.

Did you know Canada's very first female mayor was an Ontario woman? Her name was Barbara Hanley, and in nineteen thirty six she was elected mayor of Webbwood, a small town north of Sudbury. She beat her opponent eighty two votes to sixty nine. After winning, a reporter asked if she would still do her housework. She said yes — Webbwood is hardly big enough for me to give up my housework just to be mayor. For decades her achievement was forgotten. Other women were wrongly called Canada's first female mayor. She has only recently started getting the recognition she deserves.

Toronto has the oldest subway in Canada. It opened on March thirtieth, nineteen fifty four, running under Yonge Street from Union Station to Eglinton. But it almost never got built. The city first proposed a subway in nineteen ten, and voters rejected it. It came up again in the nineteen forties, and this time voters approved it — by a ratio of almost ten to one. But there was a catch. The approval was conditional on the federal government covering twenty percent of the cost. The feds promised. Then they backed out. Toronto built it anyway, entirely on its own dime, for sixty seven million dollars.

A Canadian engineer named Donald Hings invented a portable two-way radio in nineteen thirty seven. He called it a packset. It was built for mining operations in remote northern Canada. When World War Two began, the Canadian military secretly hired him to adapt it for combat. Eighteen thousand units were built for Allied troops. The device was widely considered superior to anything the Americans or British had. At a demonstration for journalists in Toronto, a soldier was asked what it does. He said — you can talk with it while you walk with it. A reporter wrote that down, and the world has called it a walkie-talkie ever since.

Casa Loma was not built by some random eccentric millionaire. It was built by the man who literally electrified Toronto. Sir Henry Pellatt founded the Toronto Electric Light Company in eighteen eighty three — the same year Thomas Edison developed steam-generated electricity. By the time he was thirty, his company had a monopoly on street lighting in Toronto. He went on to build the first hydro-generating plant at Niagara Falls. At the peak of his wealth, he controlled roughly a quarter of Canada's economy. He built Casa Loma in nineteen eleven for three and a half million dollars. Then the government seized his electric company without compensation. He died in his chauffeur's spare bedroom.

The Royal Ontario Museum has nine human mummies in its collection. Most have names, titles, even professions. But there is one adult mummy that arrived in a coffin inscribed for a male priest — and when researchers X-rayed the body, they thought it was male too. Then modern CT scans revealed it was likely female. The coffin belonged to someone, the mummy belonged to someone else, and nobody knows who either of them actually are. The mummy has been at the ROM since around nineteen oh seven, over a century, and its identity remains unknown.

In eighteen ninety two, Toronto had an ambitious commuter rail line that looped around the entire city. It was called the Belt Line. It ran twenty seven kilometres through what are now some of Toronto's most desirable neighbourhoods — Moore Park, Forest Hill, the Annex. The idea was to connect new suburban developments to downtown Union Station. It failed almost immediately. Ticket prices were too high, the real estate boom it depended on collapsed, and the country was in an economic depression. The whole thing shut down after just two years. Today, most of the old route is a walking trail called the Kay Gardner Beltline Trail.

Kingston Ontario claims to be the birthplace of hockey. The international hockey federation disagrees — they say it was Montreal. On March third, eighteen seventy five, the first officially recorded indoor hockey game was played at the Victoria Skating Rink in Montreal. It was organized by a man named James Creighton, who grew up in Nova Scotia and moved to Montreal. The game ended in chaos. Hockey players and recreational skaters got into a fight over ice time. A newspaper in Kingston reported — shins and heads were battered, benches smashed, and the lady spectators fled in confusion. Some things never change.

queue.topic: gardiner was supposed to circle toronto
queue.script: When the Gardiner Expressway opened in the nineteen fifties, it was not meant to be a standalone highway. It was just one piece of a massive ring road system that was supposed to completely encircle Toronto. The plan included an inner expressway running east-west through the Annex and Rosedale, a north-south extension through established neighbourhoods, and connections to Highway Four Hundred that would have run right through the downtown core. Residents pushed back hard. The Spadina Expressway was famously cancelled in nineteen seventy one after massive community protests. The Gardiner is what you get when a city planned for a highway network, built a third of it, and then changed its mind.

Toronto Island is not actually an island by nature. For most of its history it was a long sandy peninsula stretching nine kilometres into Lake Ontario, connected to the mainland near what is now Woodbine Avenue. People walked across it, built hotels on it, and rode horses along it. Then on April thirteenth, eighteen fifty eight, a massive storm hit from the southeast. It destroyed at least one hotel and tore a five hundred foot wide channel right through the sandbar. The city considered repairing it, then decided not to. That channel became the Eastern Gap, and a peninsula became an island overnight.

history.script: Ever notice you can see the CN Tower from almost anywhere? Because it’s so tall, it rises far above the skyline and even curves slightly with the Earth’s surface. That’s why the CN Tower becomes a reference point no matter where you are.

The CN Tower is obvious… but what’s the second tallest building in Toronto? That title goes to First Canadian Place, standing at about two hundred ninety eight meters tall. Still massive, but nowhere close to the CN Tower’s five hundred fifty three meters.

The CN Tower was basically built to get struck by lightning. Because it’s so tall, engineers designed it to safely absorb lightning strikes instead of letting them hit nearby buildings. So every time it gets hit, it’s actually protecting the rest of Toronto.

history.topic: lighting cn tower am i in danger?
history.script: If lightning hits the CN Tower while you’re nearby… are you in danger? Not really. The tower acts like a giant lightning rod, directing electricity safely into the ground. So standing near the CN Tower is actually safer than being out in the open during a storm.

The CN Tower doesn’t just get struck by lightning… it gets hit a lot. On average, lightning hits the tower around seventy five times every year. That’s because it’s the tallest structure in Toronto, making it the easiest target during storms. It’s actually designed for this, with lightning traveling safely down into the ground.
